articles.title: Concussion Basics for Sports Parents
articles.summary: How to recognize, respond to, and prevent concussions in youth sports.
articles.content: Concussions are a type of traumatic brain injury caused by a bump, blow, or jolt to the head or body that causes the brain to move rapidly back and forth. In youth sports, concussions can occur in both contact and non-contact activities. Parents should watch for symptoms such as headache, confusion, dizziness, nausea, and changes in behavior or sleep. If a concussion is suspected, the athlete should be removed from play immediately and evaluated by a medical professional. This article explains common signs and symptoms, what to do in the first 24–48 hours, when to seek emergency care, and how to talk with your child’s coach about return-to-play guidelines.

articles.title: Sideline Checklist: Recognizing Concussion Symptoms
articles.summary: Quick sideline checklist for coaches and parents to spot possible concussions.
articles.content: When an athlete takes a hard hit, coaches and parents need a simple checklist to decide what to do next. Observable signs include appearing dazed, moving clumsily, answering questions slowly, or losing consciousness. Reported symptoms include headache, pressure in the head, nausea, balance problems, or sensitivity to light and noise. This printable checklist walks you through what to look for on the sideline, when to remove a player from play, and how to document the incident so that medical professionals have accurate information.

articles.title: 2026 Return-to-Play Guidelines After Concussion
articles.summary: The latest step-by-step return-to-play protocol for youth athletes after a concussion.
articles.content: Updated for 2026, these return-to-play guidelines outline a gradual, six-step approach that starts with complete rest and ends with full game participation. Each step should last at least 24 hours and only progress if the athlete remains symptom-free. The stages include daily activities that do not provoke symptoms, light aerobic exercise, sport-specific exercise, non-contact training drills, full contact practice, and return to competition. Parents and coaches should work closely with a healthcare provider trained in concussion management and should never rush a child back to play. The article includes a printable progress tracking chart and a sample communication form to share between families, coaches, and medical providers.

articles.title: Heat Safety Tips for Summer Practices
articles.summary: Guidelines to prevent heat-related illnesses during hot-weather practices.
articles.content: Youth athletes are at increased risk for heat illness during summer practices. This guide covers how to schedule sessions during cooler parts of the day, ensure adequate hydration, recognize early signs of heat exhaustion, and modify practices when humidity is high. It also includes a sample heat policy that families and coaches can review together.

articles.title: Parent Guide to Youth Soccer Seasons
articles.summary: An overview of what to expect in fall, winter, spring, and summer soccer.
articles.content: Soccer is offered in multiple seasons with different goals: skill-building clinics, recreational leagues, and competitive travel teams. This guide explains the typical commitment, costs, and equipment needed for each season, plus questions to ask before registering your child.

articles.title: Choosing Affordable Youth Sports Programs
articles.summary: How to find quality programs that fit your family budget.
articles.content: Sports should be accessible to every child. Learn how to compare program fees, look for scholarship opportunities, and evaluate what is included in the cost (uniforms, tournaments, travel) so that you can make a sustainable choice for your family.

articles.title: Coaching Young Girls in Basketball
articles.summary: Strategies for building confidence and skills in girls basketball.
articles.content: Girls often face unique challenges in sports, including lower confidence and fewer role models. This article offers drills, communication tips, and team culture ideas to help coaches create an empowering environment for girls basketball teams.

articles.title: Fun Beginner Soccer Drills for Ages 6–10
articles.summary: Easy-to-run drills that keep new players engaged and learning.
articles.content: Introducing young players to soccer should prioritize fun, touches on the ball, and teamwork. This article describes several station-based drills with minimal equipment that are ideal for volunteer coaches and small spaces.

articles.title: Balancing Sports and Schoolwork
articles.summary: Time-management tips for student-athletes and their families.
articles.content: Busy practice and game schedules can make homework feel overwhelming. Learn how to create a weekly plan, communicate with coaches about big tests, and build study habits that travel well to tournaments and away games.

articles.title: How Our Academic Tutoring Supports Athletes
articles.summary: Overview of free and low-cost tutoring available to participants.
articles.content: Our academic tutoring program offers homework help, literacy support, and math coaching designed specifically for student-athletes. Sessions are scheduled around practice times and emphasize transferable skills like focus and goal-setting.

articles.title: Expanding Girls Sports Programs in Our Community
articles.summary: An update on new and growing opportunities for girls in sports.
articles.content: Thanks to donor support, we have added new girls basketball, soccer, and multi-sport offerings. This article highlights new teams, scholarship slots, and leadership programs specifically for girls.

articles.title: Preparing Your Family for a First 5K
articles.summary: Training and race-day tips for family-friendly 5K events.
articles.content: Running a 5K together can be a fun family goal. This article offers an 8-week beginner training plan, tips for choosing shoes, and advice for making race day positive for kids and adults alike.

articles.title: Healthy Snack Ideas for Practice and Games
articles.summary: Nutritious, kid-approved snacks for before and after activities.
articles.content: Swap sugary drinks and candy for snacks that help kids perform and recover. We provide a list of simple snacks that travel well, plus tips for organizing a parent snack schedule.

articles.title: Why Multi-Sport Play Helps Young Athletes
articles.summary: The long-term benefits of playing more than one sport.
articles.content: Research suggests that kids who sample multiple sports develop broader athletic skills and experience fewer overuse injuries. Learn how to balance multi-sport schedules without burning out.

articles.title: What to Expect at Volunteer Orientation
articles.summary: An overview of our volunteer onboarding process.
articles.content: Before stepping on the field or into a tutoring room, volunteers attend an orientation that covers child safety, organization values, and role-specific training. This article walks through the process and answers common questions.
